rs1801279.technical_report:
N-acetyltransferases, such as NAT2, are enzymes that act primarily in the liver to detoxify a
large number of chemicals, including some drugs such as amifampridine. Variants in the NAT2 gene that can affect NAT2 acetylation function are important because
of the role they play in the activation and/or deactivation of many drugs and chemicals,
including those produced by tobacco, as well as drugs composed of aromatic amines and
hydrazine that are used medicinally. In turn, the presence of deleterious polymorphisms in
NAT2 can affect an individual's risk of cancer. Certain variants in the NAT2 gene can affect the rate and extent of amifampridine
metabolism. NAT2 poor metabolizers, known as slow acetylators, are those carrying two
alleles with reduced function. In slow acetylators the drug concentrations in the body are
higher than in intermediate and fast acetylators, with one and two functional alleles,
respectively. Therefore, slow NAT2 acetylators have a higher risk of adverse effects if
treated with amifampridine. In the general population, the prevalence of poor metabolizers for NAT2 is 40-60% in
Caucasian and African-American populations, and 10-30% in Asian populations.

Amifampridine (Dosage)
Amifampridine is a potassium channel inhibitor drug used in the treatment of
myasthenic syndromes such as Lambert-Eaton syndrome. Polymorphisms
in the NAT2 gene can determine the plasma concentrations of the drug and
its genotyping can be useful for the adjustment of the appropriate dose.

The technique used is Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS 30x).

Amifampridine is a drug that acts on the nervous system for the symptomatic treatment of
Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome.
MECHANISM OF ACTION
Amifampridine blocks voltage-dependent potassium channels by prolonging the
depolarization of the presynaptic cell membrane, thus promoting calcium entry into the
nerve ending. The resulting increase in intracellular calcium concentrations facilitates
exocytosis of acetylcholine-containing vesicles, which in turn increases neuromuscular
transmission. The result is an increase in muscle strength and resting compound muscle
action potential amplitudes.
CONTRAINDICATIONS
Hypersensitivity to amifampridine; epileptic patients; patients with uncontrolled asthma;
patients with congenital cardiac QT interval syndromes.
Amifampridine is contraindicated in concomitance with sultopride, with drugs that can
prolong QTc and with drugs with narrow therapeutic margin.
Do not use during pregnancy. Not for use during lactation since it is not known whether
amifampridine is excreted in human breast milk.
PRECAUTIONS
Extreme caution should be exercised when amifampridine is prescribed in patients with
hepatic or renal insufficiency. Caution in patients at risk of epileptic seizures: if a seizure
occurs immediately discontinue treatment with amifampridine. A clinical and
electrocardiographic control should always be performed at the beginning of treatment and
once a year thereafter. Amifampridine is not indicated to treat children under 18 years of
age since there are no safety and efficacy data.
SIDE EFFECTS
Peripheral and peribuccal paresthesias; epigastralgia, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain;
sleep disorders; convulsions, anxiety, dizziness, weakness, tiredness, headache, chorea,
myoclonus; blurred vision; heart rhythm disturbances, palpitations; Raynaud's syndrome,
cold extremities; cough, bronchial hypersecretion, asthma attacks in asthmatics or with a
history of asthma; elevation of transaminases.
PHARMACOLOGICAL INTERACTIONS

 Amifampridine increases the risk of seizures with: antidepressants (tricyclics, selective
serotonin reuptake inhibitors), neuroleptics (phenothiazines and butyrophenones),
mefloquine, bupropion, tramadol.
Amifampridine increases the effect of direct or indirect cholinesterase inhibitors, and
decreases the effect of both inhibitors if amifampridine is taken concomitantly with:
mivacurium, pipercurium, suxamethonium. Extreme precautions should be taken
when administered concomitantly with drugs that are eliminated by metabolism or
active secretion.
BRAND NAMES
Firdapse®
Rusurgui®

Dose
The FDA (U.S. Food and Drug Administration) indicates in the package insert of
Rusurgui®(amifampiridine) that poor NAT2 metabolizers may present an increase in the
systemic concentration of amifampiridine and a higher risk of adverse effects. They
indicate that in NAT2 poor metabolizers, the lowest recommended starting dose should be
used and adverse reactions should be monitored.

rs4673993.technical_report:
Methotrexate is the most frequently prescribed antirheumatic drug to treat the symptoms of
rheumatoid arthritis, being even prescribed as monotherapy in many cases. Methotrexate
enters cells and glutamic acid residues are added to it, forming methotrexate polyglutamate
which promotes inhibition of the methionine, folate and adenosine pathways. It promotes
the synthesis of pririmidines and purines that are essential for exerting the antiproliferative
and anti-inflammatory properties of methotrexate. Although methotrexate is well tolerated by patients, it is estimated that one third of patients
do not respond well, due to insufficient efficacy or adverse effects. This lack of response
can be due, in part, to variations in certain genes of folate metabolism or other genes, and a
large number of pharmacogenetic studies are being carried out to obtain markers of
response to methotrexate, without much success as yet. The SLC19A1 (Solute Carrier Family 19 Member 1) gene encoding for reduced folate
transporter 1 mediates the transport of methotrexate into cells. The rs1051266
variant of SLC19A1 is the most studied and patients with the TT genotype have been
observed to have an increased response to the drug compared to CC and CT genotypes
(Chen et al., 2017). It is believed that certain variants of SLC19A1 may influence transporter
function and entry of methotrexate into cells thereby affecting treatment. Another gene of interest in the response to methotrexate is ATIC (aminoimidazole
carboxamide adenosine ribonucleotide transformylase) which codes for an enzyme involved
in purine synthesis. Methotrexate polyglutamate directly inhibits the ATIC enzyme. One of
the ATIC variants associated with methotrexate response is rs4673993 which is located in
an intron and is thought to interfere with gene transcription. Patients with CC genotype have
a greater response to methotrexate compared to patients with CT and TT genotypes (Lima
et al., 2014). It should be taken into consideration that, these variants alone cannot explain the variability
in drug response observed between patients so other genes and associated factors are
believed to be involved. In addition, further large studies in different populations are
required to confirm both associations.

Methotrexate is a drug pertaining to the antimetabolites family of drugs that presents
several indications. It is used to treat rheumatoid arthritis, severe psoriasis, certain types
of cancer (lymphomas, leukemias, uterus, breast, lung, head and neck) and is also used as
an immunosuppressant in organ transplants to prevent rejection. Methotrexate can treat
rheumatoid arthritis by decreasing the activity of the immune system. Methotrexate treats
cancer by slowing the growth of cancer cells. Methotrexate treats psoriasis by slowing the
growth of skin cells to interrupt desquamation. Methotrexate is used to prevent rejection of
the graft in transplants since it decreases the activity of the immune system.
Methotrexate is also sometimes used to treat Crohn's disease, multiple sclerosis,
autoimmune diseases, and other conditions that develop when the immune system attacks
healthy cells in the body by mistake.
ACTION MECHANISM
Antineoplastic and immunosuppressant antagonist of folic acid. Methotrexate interferes in
processes of DNA synthesis, repair and cellular replication; It can decrease the
development of malignant tissues without irreversible damage in normal tissues.
CONTRAINDICATIONS
Pregnancy, breastfeeding, existing or recent chickenpox, herpes zoster. Should be
evaluated the risk-benefit ratio in patients suffering ascites, gastrointestinal obstruction,
peritoneal or pleural effusion, renal dysfunction, depression of the bone marrow, history
of gout, kidney stones, liver dysfunction, infection, peptic ulcer and ulcerative colitis.
Caution should also be exercised in patients undergoing treatment with cytotoxic drugs or
radiation therapy.
SIDE EFFECTS
Many adverse reactions are unavoidable and represent the pharmacological action of
methotrexate; for example, leucopenia and thrombocytopenia, which are used as indicators
of the effectiveness of the medication and facilitate the adjustment of the individual
dosage. The development of side effects require medical attention only if they persist or
are annoying: anorexia, nausea and vomiting. The side effects that are more frequent and
require medical attention are: hematemesis, diarrhea, hematuria, arthralgia and edema.
With intrathecal administration may appear: blurred vision, confusion, dizziness,
drowsiness, headaches, seizures or unusual fatigue. Alopecia does not require medical
attention. The development of certain side effects can indicate possible toxicity of the
central nervous system: blurred vision, confusion, dizziness and headaches. Side effects
are more likely to occur in very young or elderly patients.

 When used as an antineoplastic the side effects that can be developed are the following:
Leukocytopenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia; headache, fatigue, drowsiness, paresthesia;
pulmonary complications due to interstitial alveolitis/pneumonitis (typical symptoms:
malaise, irritating and dry cough, respiratory distress that progresses to dyspnea at rest,
chest pain, fever); loss of appetite, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, inflammation and
ulceration of the mucous membrane of the mouth and throat, stomatitis, dyspepsia,
diarrhea; rash, erythema, pruritus; increase in liver enzymes (ALAT, ASAT, alkaline
phosphatase and bilirubin).
If an overdose of methotrexate occurs, it should be rescued with folinic acid by intravenous
infusion in doses of up to 75 mg in the first 12 hours followed by folinic acid 15 mg oral or
intravenous every 6 hours at least 4 doses, until the serum levels of methotrexate are less
than 5x10-7 M. A treatment with intravenous and intrathecal carboxypeptidase G2 has been
published to accelerate the clearance of methotrexate.
PHARMACOLOGIC INTERACTIONS
The simultaneous administration of intrathecal methotrexate with acyclovir can produce
neurological abnormalities. Methotrexate increases the risk of hepatotoxicity with alcohol
or hepatotoxic medications. Methotrexate increases the concentration of uric acid in the
blood, so it is necessary to readjust the dosage of allopurinol, colchicine or probenecid.
Methotrexate increases the anticoagulant activity and the risk of hemorrhages by reducing
the hepatic synthesis of the coagulation factors, for this reason the dose of anticoagulants
derived from coumarin needs to be regulated. The use of NSAIDs increases the risk of
agranulocytosis (phenylbutazone). Asparaginase can block the effects of methotrexate by
inhibiting cell replication. The administration of cytarabine after initiation of therapy with
methotrexate may produce a synergistic cytotoxic effect. Folic acid can interfere with the
antifolate effects of methotrexate. Oral kanamycin increases the absorption of oral
methotrexate. Oral neomycin decreases the absorption of oral methotrexate. Probenecid
and salicylates can inhibit renal excretion of methotrexate. Pyrimethamine, triamterene or
trimethoprim may increase the toxic effects of methotrexate due to their similar actions as
folic acid antagonists.
If you wish to know all the substances that we analyze in our DNA test, please consult the
pharmacological compatibility or pharmacogenetic section.

rs1142345.technical_report:
It is believed that the adverse effect of cisplatin-induced hearing loss is due to its
interference with signal transduction in the cochlea, which is part of the inner ear. Studies
suggest that cisplatin could cause apoptosis in the outer hair cells of the organ of Corti, the
spiral ganglion, and the stria vascularis. However, the underlying mechanism that triggers
apoptosis in hair cells is not fully understood. Certain factors can influence the risk of
developing cisplatin-induced ototoxicity, such as cisplatin dosage, age, presence of preexisting hearing impairments, use alongside aminoglycosides, and cranial irradiation. These
factors have a cumulative effect on the risk. In addition, variation in genes involved in the
metabolism, transport, and targets of cisplatin has been observed to be another important
factor. Thiopurine S-methyltransferase (TPMT) is an enzyme that serves a key function
in the metabolism of cisplatin and other chemotherapeutic drugs. evidence suggests that
genetic polymorphisms in TPMT contribute to the risk of cisplatin-induced ototoxicity in
children. Patients carrying at least one loss-of-function TPMT allele respond worse to
cisplatin chemotherapy than those patients carrying both functional alleles. Currently, the
CPNDS (Canadian Pharmacogenomics Network for Drug Safety) pharmacogenomic clinical
guideline recommends genotyping for 3 of the TPMT variants that define the loss-offunction alleles *3A, *3B, *3C, and the loss-of-function allele TPMT*2 in pediatric patients
who are to be treated with cisplatin. The study of these variants explains approximately 25%
of cases of cisplatin-induced ototoxicity and has a high positive predictive value (92%). Other less frequent variants in TPMT have been described that could influence its enzymatic
activity and are not included in the CPNDS guideline recommendations or in this analysis.

Cisplatin, or cis-diamminedichloroplatinum(II), is a well-known metal-based
chemotherapeutic drug used in the standard treatment of various solid cancers such as
testicular, breast, ovarian, head and neck, bladder, lung, cervical, stomach, melanoma,
neuroblastoma, osteosarcoma, and lymphomas. Depending on the type of cancer, cisplatin
can be used alone or with other compounds such as, for example, paclitaxel, in the
treatment of ovarian cancer.
Two of the main limitations of cisplatin use are treatment resistance and toxicity. Multiple
platinum compounds have been produced to minimize the development of treatment
resistance and improve pharmacokinetic properties. Some of the platinum compounds
alternative to cisplatin are carboplatin, oxaliplatin, satraplatin, and nedaplatin.
The main toxicities derived from cisplatin treatment are nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity,
hepatotoxicity, gastrointestinal toxicity, and neurotoxicity.
MECHANISM OF ACTION
The main mechanism through which cisplatin exerts its action is the interaction with
genomic and mitochondrial DNA and the generation of damage in its structure, activating
apoptosis (cell death).
CONTRAINDICATIONS
Cisplatin should be avoided in patients with hypersensitivity to cisplatin or other platinumbased compounds, renal dysfunction, dehydration, myelosuppression, hearing impairment,
or neuropathy caused by cisplatin.
SIDE EFFECTS
Cisplatin is a medication that can cause toxicity and multiple adverse effects including:
infections, sepsis, hematological disorders (leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and anemia),
low blood sodium levels, venous thromboembolism, peripheral neuropathy, hearing loss,
vertigo, cardiac arrhythmia, erythema, skin ulcer, and localized edema.
Cisplatin ototoxicity is one of the most frequent adverse reactions, manifesting as
permanent bilateral hearing loss, affecting 10-25% of adults and 26-90% of children.
TRADE NAMES
Platinol®

 Platinol-AQ®

rs17863783.technical_report:
There is clear evidence of the role of pharmacogenetics and pharmacogenomics in
susceptibility to cardiotoxicity induced by drugs used for cancer treatment. The most
studied are the anthracyclines daunorubicin and doxorubicin. Cardiotoxic effects can range from coronary heart disease, pericardial disease, valvular
heart disease, cardiomyopathy, and arrhythmias. Congestive heart failure (also called heart
failure) is the main cardiomyopathy induced by these chemotherapeutic agents. In addition to genetics, there are other factors that can influence the risk of anthracyclineinduced cardiotoxicity, which have a cumulative effect, such as concomitant treatment with
radiotherapy, high doses of anthracyclines, shorter intravenous perfusion time, the presence
of cardiovascular diseases, dyslipidemia, age over 65 years and female sex. Currently, the CPNDS (Canadian Pharmacogenomics Network for Drug Safety)
pharmacogenomic clinical guideline recommends genotyping of the three variants RARG
rs2229774, SLC28A3 rs7853758 and UGT1A6*4 (rs17863783). Increased RARG activity decreases the expression of the topoisomerase II gene (TOP2B),
and it has been shown that TOP2B reduction has a cardioprotective effect. The RARG
rs2229774 variant reduces the ability of RARG to decrease TOP2B expression, which
negatively affects cardioprotection. The SLC28A3 gene encodes an anthracycline transporter protein, the SLC28A3 rs7853758
variant produces a decrease in gene expression, thereby reducing the entry of
anthracyclines into cardiac cells. Therefore, the rs7853758 variant is considered to have a
protective effect against anthracycline-induced cardiotoxicity. UGT1A6 is a UDP-glucuronosyltransferase enzyme that participates in the detoxification of
drugs and other xenobiotics. The rs17863783 variant represents the UGT1A6*4 allele, which
is an allele that reduces UGT1A6-mediated glucuronidation activity. It has been observed
that the presence of this variant increases the amount of cardiotoxic metabolites, thereby
increasing the likelihood of anthracycline-induced cardiotoxicity.

Anthracyclines have been used for over 60 years in cancer treatment and have saved many
lives. However, a significant limitation of their use is cardiotoxicity.
Daunorubicin is an anthracycline used to treat acute myeloid leukemia and acute lymphoid
leukemia in children, although it can also be used in adult patients.
Doxorubicin is used to treat certain types of cancer of the urinary bladder, breast, lung,
stomach, and ovary, Ewing's sarcoma, Hodgkin's lymphoma (Hodgkin's disease) and nonHodgkin's lymphoma, Wilms' tumor, thyroid cancer, neuroblastoma, and certain leukemias.
MECHANISM OF ACTION
Anthracyclines alter DNA by interfering with topoisomerase, an enzyme essential for DNA
replication and synthesis, thereby causing cell cycle arrest and programmed cell death.
CONTRAINDICATIONS
Treatment with anthracyclines is contraindicated in cases of hypersensitivity to
anthracyclines and anthracenediones, persistent myelosuppression, severe infections,
severe hepatic impairment, heart failure, recent myocardial infarction, severe arrhythmias,
previous treatments with the same or another cardiotoxic anthracycline, pregnancy,
and lactation.
SIDE EFFECTS
Nausea, vomiting, and stomach pain
Mouth and throat sores
Weight loss or gain
Muscle weakness
Hair loss
Tingling in the hands and feet
You should contact your doctor immediately if you develop hives, rash, itching, difficulty
breathing or swallowing, or seizures.
As mentioned, cardiotoxicity is an adverse effect that limits its use and can occur acutely
(during treatment), early (during the first year of treatment), or late (years or decades
later). Therefore, close monitoring of patients should be carried out before, during, and

 after cancer therapy. The determination of LVEF (left ventricular ejection fraction) by
echocardiography or with the technique of multiple-gated acquisition (MUGA) scan and
magnetic resonance imaging are tests that are performed to assess for cardiotoxicity.
Biochemical markers that can be used for early detection of cardiac damage are troponin T
and N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide (NT-proBNP).
BRAND NAMES
Adriamycin®
Cerubidine®
Doxil®
Myocet®
Rubex®
Vyxeos®

rs530517316.technical_report:
Pseudocholinesterase is a plasma enzyme produced in the liver that metabolizes anesthetic
drugs such as succinylcholine and mivacurium, as well as ester-type local anesthetics such
as cocaine. Genetically, BChE deficiency is an autosomal recessive trait found in 1 in 100,000 people of
European descent and varying across populations. The incidence in Jewish communities is
approximately 1 in 400 individuals. Some variants of the BCHE gene result in reduced enzymatic activity and others in a
decrease in the amount of circulating active enzyme. In people with a normal functional
enzyme, these drugs are rapidly metabolized, resulting in a short action (less than 10
minutes for succinylcholine). However, if there is BChE deficiency, the drug takes longer to
be metabolized and eliminated from the body, prolonging neuromuscular paralysis. The severity of this adverse effect depends on the genotype; carriers of one copy of a
heterozygous BCHE variant exhibit moderate block, while carriers of two copies or
homozygotes may experience muscle paralysis for more than 2 hours. The most common variant in Caucasians is the atypical (A) variant rs1799807 (c.293A>G,
p.Asp70Gly) followed by the Kalow (K) variant rs1803274 (c.1699G>A, p. Ala539Tyr). Less
common variants of the BCHE gene include three fluoride-resistant or F variants such as
rs28933389 (c.812C>T, p.Thr271Met), rs28933390 (c.1253G>T, p.Gly418Val)
and rs121918557 (c.1072T>A, p.Leu358Ile) and silent variants called S variants. Some S variants lead to drastic reductions in BCHE activity such as rs104893684
(c.1004T>C, p.Leu335Pro). It is important to consider that BChE deficiency can be acquired and not due to genetic
alterations. Prolongation of neuromuscular block due to pseudocholinesterase deficiency
can be influenced by other factors such as increasing age, pregnancy, severe renal or
hepatic disease, cancer, burn injuries, malnutrition, and drug interactions.

Mivacurium is a short-acting, non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agent used as an
adjunct in general anesthesia to relax skeletal muscles. This medication works by
competitively binding to cholinergic receptors at the motor endplate, blocking the action of
acetylcholine, and consequently inhibiting neuromuscular transmission.
Meanwhile, succinylcholine (or suxamethonium) is an ultra-short-acting muscle relaxant
belonging to the class of depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agents, mainly used to
facilitate tracheal intubation and muscle relaxation in surgical procedures.
Both mivacurium and succinylcholine are rapidly metabolized by enzymatic hydrolysis
catalyzed by the enzyme pseudocholinesterase or butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) in plasma.
However, a rare but potentially serious adverse effect is prolonged apnea after
administration of these agents under general anesthesia. This risk is associated with
defects in the BChE enzyme, which may be caused by mutations in the BCHE gene that
encodes this enzyme. Generally, people with BChE deficiency do not show symptoms until
they receive drugs such as mivacurium or succinylcholine.
If you have a family history of BChE deficiency or a family member who has experienced
problems related to anesthesia, it is essential to inform your doctor before undergoing any
procedure that requires anesthesia.
TRADE NAMES
Mivacron® (mivacurium)
Anectine® (succinylcholine)
Quelicin® (succinylcholine)

rs1799732.technical_report:
According to several studies, DRD2 has been shown to be an important gene in the
development of schizophrenia. DRD2, encodes for the dopamine receptor type 2 which is a
relevant presynaptic receptor. The rs1800497 variant, also known as c.2137G>A or Taq1A is
located in the ANKK1(ankyrin repeat and kinase domain containing 1) gene. This variant is
located 10 Kb from the DRD2 gene and correlates with lower DRD2 expression. Taq1A has been studied for its importance in the response to antipsychotics and also
for its association with substance abuse, alcohol dependence, eating disorders and
smoking cessation. The rs1800497 AA (ANKK1) genotype is associated with an increased risk of Headache
when treated with olanzapine, quetiapine or risperidone in healthy individuals compared to
the GG genotype (Lopez-Rodriguez et al., 2013). Another well-studied DRD2 variant is -141C
Ins/Del or c.-486_-485insC (rs1799732) located in the 5 promoter region of the gene. This
polymorphism may influence the density of D2 receptors in the striatum of healthy
volunteers not treated with antipsychotics. It has been observed, that schizophrenic
patients carrying the Del allele might have a poor response to olanzapine and risperidone
(Zhang and Malhotra. 2011). Finally, we analyzed the rs7997012 variant of the HTR2A gene have also been correlated
with antipsychotic treatment response and side effects. It is important to take into consideration that there are other factors that may influence
the response to olanzapine treatment. At present, the genetic variants analyzed here
require further studies to confirm previous relationships before they can be employed in
clinical practice.

Olanzapine is in a class of medications called atypical antipsychotics, structurally similar
to clozapine and having the same mechanism of action. Olanzapine binds to alpha-1,
dopaminergic, histaminic H1, muscarinic and serotoninergic (type 5-HT2) receptors.
Compared to the typical antipsychotics, olanzapine has more reduced cardiovascular
effects and induces less hyperprolactinemia and extrapyramidal reactions. From a clinical
point of view, olanzapine is effective in treating the symptoms of schizophrenia and is also
used in treating acute mania with bipolar disorder, and in reducing agitation and other
psychotic symptoms in dementia.
Olanzapine is an atypical neuroleptic with effects similar to other neuroleptics on is used
to treat both negative and positive symptoms of schizophrenia, and is also effective for
persistent positive symptoms. It works by changing the activity of certain natural
substances in the brain.
Weight gain is one of the most important side effects associated with antipsychotic drug
treatment. It has a negative influence on one's health (associated with diabetes, heart
disease, and other serious disorders) and the personal well-being of the patient with a
reduced fulfillment of treatment and the possible risk of recurrence of the illness. The
importance of this problem was shown by Fontaine et al. who found that clozapine, in spite
of preventing suicide in 492 out of 100,000 schizophrenic patients treated over a 10 year
period, could also be considered responsible for 416 deaths caused by the consequences
of weight gain induced by antipsychotics.
Among the atypical neuroleptic drugs, olanzapine and clozapine have been associated with
a greater weight gain, with an average gain of 5 kg over short-term treatment and 6.7 kg
over long-term treatment.
Genetic factors are believed to play an important role in an individual's response to
antipsychotic drugs, both in the benefits of treatment and in the susceptibility to side
effects. The variations in the gene sequence, called polymorphism, could influence the
properties of the coded proteins and the results in individual response to the drugs.
If you wish to know all the substances that we analyze in our DNA test, please consult the
pharmacological compatibility or pharmacogenetic section.

rs6280.technical_report:
Olanzapine is transformed into its main metabolite, olanzapine-10-N-glucuronide, by the
enzyme UGT1A4. However, other enzymes are involved in its transformation, one of the
main ones being CYP1A2, while CYP2D6 plays a minor role. research indicates that
patients presenting two copies of the CYP1A2*1F allele which is defined by the presence of
the -163C>A, c.-9-154C>A or rs762551 variant respond worse to olanzapine treatment than
patients who do not carry the CYP1A2*1F allele. Another gene that could be related to olanzapine response is ABCB1 or MDR1 which
encodes for a transporter protein called P-gp or P-glycoprotein. This transporter is
expressed in multiple tissues and transports xenobiotics out of the tissue or organ where it
is expressed. Olanzapine is one of the substances it can transport and it has been shown
that variants in the gene encoding it, such as rs1045642 or c.3435T>C can influence the
response to treatment. The Ser9Gly or rs6280 variant is a polymorphism located in the DRD3 gene (dopamine D3
receptor). It appears that olanzapine interacts with this receptor and therefore, variations in
its sequence can affect the activity of olanzapine. research indicates that the presence of
the T or Ser allele is associated with a higher probability of worse response to olanzapine
treatment and greater weight gain. In the study by Adams et al. it was observed that
olanzapine-treated patients with CC genotype for rs6280 in the DRD3 gene more efficiently
decreased their symptoms compared to patients with CT or TT genotypes. The DRD2 gene encodes for the dopaminergic D2 receptor. Mutations in this gene have been
associated with schizophrenia and also with response to olanzapine. One of the most
studied variants is rs1799978 or A-241G, the presence of one copy of the C allele in patients
with schizophrenia is associated with a faster response to olanzapine. Another well-studied
variant in this same gene is a small deletion called rs1799732, c.-486_-485insC, or -141C
Ins/Del that has been linked to a lower response to multiple antipsychotic drugs. It should be kept in mind that the biomarkers analyzed here require confirmation in larger
studies covering different populations and that, in addition, there are other factors that may
interfere with response to olanzapine treatment. Therefore, these markers are not yet used
in clinical practice.

Olanzapine is an antipsychotic agent, chemically related to clozapine and with the same
mechanism of action. Olanzapine binds to alpha-1, dopaminergic, histamine H1,
muscarinic and serotonergic (5-HT2 type) receptors. Compared to typical antipsychotics,
olanzapine shows reduced cardiovascular effects and induces less hyperprolactinemia and
extrapyramidal reactions. From a clinical point of view, olanzapine is effective in the
treatment of schizophrenia symptoms and is also used in the treatment of acute mania
with bipolar disorders and to reduce agitation and other psychotic symptoms of dementia.
When olanzapine is used, therapeutic failure may occur in some patients due to the
presence of adverse effects or because the treatment is not effective and good symptom
control is not achieved. The presence of certain variants in genes involved in its
metabolism, transport and receptors involved in the response could explain part of the
variability observed among patients.

Zydis®

rs3087374.technical_report:
VPA is one of the most widely used antiepileptic drugs worldwide to treat the manic phases
of bipolar disorder and also in other psychiatric disorders. Recently, VPA, alone or in
combination with other anti-tumor drugs, has started to be used for the treatment of several
cancers. It has also been shown that it may have a neuroprotective effect in patients with
Alzheimer's disease. Although VPA is generally well tolerated, research indicates that exceptionally it can
produce severe hepatotoxicity. This adverse effect correlates with the presence
of variants in the POLG gene that codes for the catalytic subunit of mitochondrial
DNA polymerase. More than 150 different pathogenic variants have been described in POLG that can produce
multiple clinical phenotypes such as Alpers-Huttenlocher syndrome. Among them, the most
frequent is rs113994095, also called c.1399G> A or A467T, which significantly reduces
enzyme activity to only 4% of normal activity. Another variant that markedly decreases
activity is rs113994097, c.2243G>C or W748S. Stewart et al. conducted a prospective study in patients from different centers in the United
States included in the Drug Induced Liver Injury Network (DILIN) from 2004 to 2008. This
study demonstrated that the presence of a single copy of a pathogenic variant in POLG
could lead to liver damage during treatment with VPA. When there is suspicion that a patient may have a mitochondrial disease (e.g. AlpersHuttenlocher syndrome, autosomal recessive inheritance) caused by mutations in POLG,
the gene should be analyzed before VPA is used. Treatment with VPA is contraindicated in
these patients. The detection of variants in the POLG gene can be helpful in deciding whether to use VPA as
an antiepileptic or bipolar disorder treatment, as well as in adjusting the dose of VPA in
patients with prolonged treatment while minimizing the risk of liver damage. Recent studies,
such as the one carried out by Harju et al. suggest that it could also be of interest to know
whether a patient carries pathogenic variants in POLG in therapies combining VPA with
antitumor drugs.

Valproic acid (VPA), also known as sodium valproate, magnesium valproate, or simply
valproate, is an antiepileptic drug and mood stabilizer used in the treatment of conditions
such as epilepsy or bipolar disorder. VPA increases brain GABA levels, decreases excitatory
amino acid levels, and modifies potassium conductance.
Valproic acid is used to treat disorders such as epilepsy and bipolar disorder. Although it is
very effective in controlling seizures, its use must be carefully monitored due to side
effects and interactions with other medications.
If you wish to know all the substances we analyze in our DNA test, consult the drug
compatibility section or pharmacogenomics.

CONTRAINDICATIONS
Hypersensitivity to sodium valproate.
Acute hepatitis, chronic hepatitis, personal or family history of severe hepatitis, hepatic
porphyria, previous or current liver disease and/or severe liver or pancreatic dysfunction,
branched-chain amino acid or urea cycle metabolism disorders, known mitochondrial
disorders caused by mutations in the nuclear gene encoding the mitochondrial enzyme
polymerase gamma, such as Alpers-Huttenlocher syndrome. It is contraindicated in
children under 2 years of age suspected of having a polymerase gamma-related disorder.

PRECAUTIONS
Renal insufficiency. In systemic lupus erythematosus, assess benefit-risk. Severe liver
failure has been reported, with maximum risk in infants and children under 3 years of age
with brain injury, mental retardation, or metabolic or genetic degenerative disease: evaluate
liver function before starting treatment and monitor it periodically during the first 6 months
of treatment.
In children under 3 years of age, monotherapy is recommended, and concomitant use with
salicylates should be avoided due to the risk of liver toxicity. Discontinue in case of
pancreatitis. Perform hematological tests before starting treatment, before surgical
intervention, and in case of spontaneous bruises or hemorrhages.
Do not consume alcohol. In case of suspected urea cycle enzyme deficiency, perform
metabolic examinations.

 Possible weight gain at the start of treatment. Changes in treatment or
gradual discontinuation.
Do not administer to girls, adolescent women, women of childbearing age, and pregnant
women unless alternative treatments are not effective or tolerated; the teratogenic
potential and the risk of developing developmental disorders in children in utero are high.
Women of childbearing potential must use an effective contraceptive method, and the risks
in case of pregnancy should be explained to them. If pregnancy occurs, an assessment of
the benefits and risks of continuing treatment should be made, considering other
therapeutic alternatives.
Monitor for signs of suicidal ideation and behavior.
In patients suspected of or presenting with mitochondrial disease (clinical signs of these
underlying mitochondrial diseases caused by mitochondrial DNA mutations, as well as the
nuclear gene encoding polymerase gamma, may be triggered or worsened), perform
polymerase gamma mutation tests in accordance with current clinical practice for the
diagnostic evaluation of such disorders.

SIDE EFFECTS
Valproic acid can cause anemia, thrombocytopenia, weight gain, tremor, extrapyramidal
disorders, stupor, somnolence, seizures, memory failure, headache, nystagmus, dizziness
after injection, deafness, nausea, abdominal pain, diarrhea, hypersensitivity, alopecia
(transient, dose-related), hyponatremia, hemorrhage, liver injury, dysmenorrhea,
confusional state, aggression, agitation, and attention disorders. In some cases, cognitive
function may be impaired, especially with long-term treatment.

DRUG INTERACTIONS
VPA potentiates the effect of neuroleptics, MAOIs, antidepressants, and benzodiazepines.
VPA intake increases plasma concentrations of phenobarbital, free phenytoin, primidone,
carbamazepine, lamotrigine, zidovudine, nimodipine, and ethosuximide.
Serum concentrations of valproic acid are decreased by: phenytoin, phenobarbital,
carbamazepine, and carbapenems.
Serum concentrations of valproic acid are increased by: felbamate, cimetidine, fluoxetine,
and erythromycin.
VPA decreases the clearance of felbamate. There is a risk of hemorrhage if prescribed
concomitantly with anticoagulants and ASA.
The antibiotic mefloquine is contraindicated with the use of valproic acid due to the risk of
seizures, which can aggravate the patient's condition.

 Liver toxicity exacerbated by alcohol.
Adjust dose if prescribed concomitantly with rifampicin.
Risk of encephalopathy and/or hyperammonemia if taken concomitantly with topiramate.
VPA increases propofol exposure, so it is recommended to reduce the propofol dose during
valproic treatment.
VPA intake may give false positives in ketone body excretion tests.

BRAND NAMES
Depakine®
Valproic acid®.
